31: hinei yamim baim neum YHWH vekharati et-beit Yisrael veet-beit Yehudah berit hadasha (hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum Yhwh wĕkārattî ʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-bêt yĕhûdāh bĕrît ḥădāšāh): 'Behold the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.' Berit hadasha (new covenant): the only occurrence of this exact phrase in the OT. Hadash (new) can mean 'renewed' (as in the new moon, hodesh) or 'qualitatively different.' Jeremiah's contrast makes it the latter: 'not like the covenant I made with their fathers ... which they broke' (v. 32). The new covenant is distinguished by three characteristics: (1) internalized law (v. 33: on the heart, not stone); (2) universal direct knowledge of YHWH (v. 34: no longer 'know the LORD'); (3) permanent forgiveness (v. 34: I will remember their sin no more).
1: Bāʿēt hahîʾ (bāʿēt hahîʾ), "at that time," opens ch31 as a direct continuation of the promise begun in ch30. The phrase functions as a temporal bracket connecting the new covenant oracle to the preceding reversal of fortunes. YHWH declares himself lēʾlōhîm (lēʾlōhîm) — God — to kol mišpĕḥôt yiśrāʾēl, all the families/clans of Israel, not just Judah. The breadth is programmatic: the restoration encompasses the whole covenant people, including the northern kingdom carried away by Assyria.
2: Māṣāʾ ḥēn bammidbar (māṣāʾ ḥēn bammidbar): the exodus wilderness becomes the paradigm for divine grace in judgment. The survivors of the sword (śĕrîdê ḥāreḇ) find grace (ḥēn) not as earned status but as unexpected gift. Hālôk lĕhargîʿô (hālôk lĕhargîʿô), "going to give him rest," uses the Hiphil of rāgaʿ (to give rest, to quiet). The wilderness, normally a place of death and testing, becomes the space of grace — a Hosea 2 pattern ("I will allure her into the wilderness and speak tenderly").
3: The pivotal theological statement: ʾahăḇat ʿôlām ʾăhăḇtîḵ (ʾahăḇat ʿôlām ʾăhăḇtîḵ) — "with an everlasting love I have loved you." The noun ʿôlām (long duration, eternity) modifies the love-action absolutely: this is no conditional or seasonal affection but the settled disposition of YHWH toward Israel from of old (mērāḥôq, "from far away/long ago"). Mĕšaḵtîḵ ḥāsed (mĕšaḵtîḵ ḥāsed): "I have drawn you with steadfast love/ḥesed." The verb māšaḵ (to draw, pull) combined with ḥesed is unusual — YHWH's covenant loyalty is portrayed as the cord by which he draws the exiled people back. John 6:44 ("no one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him") uses helkō in a directly analogous theological move.
4: Tîḇnēḵ wĕniḇnêt (tîḇnēḵ wĕniḇnêt): "I will build you up and you will be built" — the Qal imperfect with the Niphal passive creates a divine-human synergy: YHWH's action produces the community's rebuilt state. Bĕtûlat yiśrāʾēl (bĕtûlat yiśrāʾēl), "virgin Israel," is not a critique of moral status but a term of tender address recalling the nation before the violations of exile. The image of women taking up tambourines (tuppîm) to join the dance (mĕḥôl hāmĕśaḥăqîm) echoes Miriam's victory celebration (Exod 15:20), placing restoration in the typological pattern of exodus-deliverance.
5: Nāṭĕʿû kĕrāmîm bĕhārê šōmĕrôn (nāṭĕʿû kĕrāmîm bĕhārê šōmĕrôn): vineyards replanted on the hills of Samaria signals the inclusion of the northern kingdom (Ephraim/Israel) in the restoration — not merely Judah. Nāṭĕʿû ḥillĕlû (nāṭĕʿû ḥillĕlû): "those who plant will also enjoy/use" (Piel of ḥālal, to begin as common/unconsecrated after the three-year waiting period, Lev 19:23-25). The allusion to the firstfruits law implies a stable, normalized covenant life has returned — planting carries through to harvest without the interruption of war or exile.
6: Nōṣĕrîm bĕhar ʾEprayim (nōṣĕrîm bĕhar ʾEprayim): "watchmen on the hills of Ephraim" — the term nōṣĕr (Qal participle of nāṣar, to watch) normally refers to military sentinels or lookouts over vineyards. Here they call pilgrims to worship rather than warning of danger. Qûmû wĕnaʿăleh ṣiyyôn (qûmû wĕnaʿăleh ṣiyyôn): the pilgrimage call "Rise up! Let us go up to Zion" is the ʿaliyyâ vocabulary of the Psalms of Ascent (Ps 120-134). The convergence of Ephraim and Judah in a single pilgrimage to Zion marks the healing of the north-south schism that began with Rehoboam.
7: Ronnû lĕyaʿăqōḇ śimḥâ (ronnû lĕyaʿăqōḇ śimḥâ): "Shout with joy for Jacob" — rānan is the exultant, ringing cry of joy that belongs to the Psalter's celebration vocabulary. Ṣahlû bĕrōʾš haggôyim (ṣahlû bĕrōʾš haggôyim): "shout at the head of the nations" — the restored Israel does not hide its praise; the proclamation is public, international witness. Hôšaʿ YHWH ʾet-ʿammeḵā ʾet šĕʾērît yiśrāʾēl (hôšaʿ YHWH ʾet-ʿammeḵā): the imperative to save echoes the hôšîʿāh nāʾ of Ps 118:25 — the same liturgical cry that greets Jesus at the triumphal entry ("Hosanna," Matt 21:9).
8: Hinnĕnî mēbîʾ ôtām mēʾereṣ ṣāpôn (hinnĕnî mēbîʾ ôtām): the emphatic hinnēnî ("behold me") announces YHWH's direct personal action in the return. The gathering (qibbēṣ) is universal: ʾiwwēr wĕpissēaḥ hārâ wĕyōlĕdet yaḥdāw (ʾiwwēr wĕpissēaḥ hārâ wĕyōlĕdet) — blind, lame, pregnant, and laboring women. These are precisely those who by human capacity are least able to make a desert march. Their inclusion reverses the values of the ban on entering the sanctuary (Lev 21:18; 2 Sam 5:8) — in YHWH's restoration, the excluded are specifically gathered. Qāhāl gādôl (qāhāl gādôl), "a great assembly," is the term for the covenant assembly of Israel.
9: Biḇkî yāḇōʾû ûḇĕtaḥănûnîm ʾôḇîlēm (biḇkî yāḇōʾû): they come with weeping — not weeping of despair but of repentance and profound relief, as the Hiphil ʾôḇîlēm ("I will lead them") makes clear: YHWH himself leads the procession. ʾEl naḥălê māyim bĕdereḵ yāšār lōʾ yikkāšlû bāh (ʾel naḥălê māyim): streams of water on a level path — the exodus/wilderness imagery inverted; instead of waterlessness and stumbling, YHWH provides both the path and the sustaining water. The climax: kî-hāyîtî lĕyiśrāʾēl lĕʾāḇ ûʾEprayim bĕḵôrî hûʾ (kî-hāyîtî lĕyiśrāʾēl lĕʾāḇ) — "For I have become a father to Israel, and Ephraim is my firstborn." The designation bĕḵôr (firstborn) for Ephraim over Manasseh recalls Jacob's reversal (Gen 48:19); more fundamentally, it echoes Exod 4:22 ("Israel is my firstborn son") and grounds the paternal claim of YHWH as the basis for filial restoration.
10: Šimʿû dĕḇar-YHWH gôyim (šimʿû dĕḇar-YHWH gôyim): the proclamation extends to the nations — the restoration of Israel is a matter of international witness. Mĕzārēh yiśrāʾēl yĕqabbĕṣennû ûšĕmārô kĕrōʿeh ʿedrô (mĕzārēh yiśrāʾēl yĕqabbĕṣennû): "He who scattered Israel will gather him, and he will watch over him as a shepherd watches his flock." The same agent who scattered now gathers — the scattering was punitive, the gathering is redemptive, but the shepherd (rōʿeh) image is the same continuous care. Jesus's self-identification as the good shepherd (John 10:11) and his mission to gather the scattered sheep of the house of Israel (Matt 15:24) are direct fulfillments of this restoration-shepherd expectation.
11: Kî-pādâ YHWH ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ wĕgĕʾālô miyyad ḥāzāq mimmennû (kî-pādâ YHWH ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ wĕgĕʾālô): two key redemption terms in parallel: pādâ (ransom, to pay the price of liberation — a commercial/legal term) and gāʾal (kinsman-redeemer act, to act as family-protector who retrieves what was lost). YHWH acts as both ransomer and kinsman-redeemer. Miyyad ḥāzāq mimmennû (miyyad ḥāzāq mimmennû): "from the hand of one too strong for him" — Jacob/Israel could not free himself from Babylon's grip; the deliverance is entirely YHWH's initiative and power. Paul uses pādâ/apolytrōsis and gāʾal/exagorazō vocabulary directly for Christ's redemptive work (Rom 3:24; Gal 3:13).
12: Wĕbāʾû wĕrinnĕnû biḇmôt ṣiyyôn (wĕbāʾû wĕrinnĕnû biḇmôt ṣiyyôn): the pilgrimage song reaches its destination — Zion's heights. Wĕnāhărû ʾel-ṭûḇ YHWH (wĕnāhărû ʾel-ṭûḇ YHWH): the unusual verb nāhar (to flow, to stream — normally of rivers flowing toward the sea) pictures the people streaming toward YHWH's goodness as rivers flow; a radiant face (nāhar also means to shine/be radiant, Isa 60:5) captures the dual meaning. Wĕhāytâ napšām kĕgan rāweh (kĕgan rāweh): "their being will be like a well-watered garden" — the nephesh as the whole person, fully satisfied; Ps 23:1-3 uses the same imagery of YHWH as the shepherd who leads beside still waters and restores the nephesh.
13: Ûḇĕtûlâ tiśmaḥ bĕmāḥôl ûḇaḥûrîm ûzĕqēnîm yaḥdāw (ûḇĕtûlâ tiśmaḥ bĕmāḥôl): the communal celebration encompasses all generations — young women, young men, and elders rejoice together. Wĕhāpaḵtî ʾeḇlām lĕśāśôn (wĕhāpaḵtî ʾeḇlām lĕśāśôn): "I will turn their mourning into joy" — the Qal perfect of hāpaḵ (to overturn, reverse) is the same verb used for Sodom's overthrow (Gen 19:25); here divine reversal works positively. The consolation (niḥamtîm) and rejoicing in place of sorrow (śamaḥtîm miyyĕgônām) becomes the NT pattern of divine comfort throughout (2 Cor 1:3-4; Rev 21:4).
14: Wĕribbêtî nefesh hakkōhănîm dāšen (wĕribbêtî nefesh hakkōhănîm dāšen): the priests are filled with fatness (dāšen — rich abundance, the fat of offerings representing fullness of blessing). The priestly abundance is YHWH's own initiative (wĕribbêtî, I will satisfy them abundantly). Wĕʾammî ʾet-ṭûḇî yiśbāʿû (wĕʾammî ʾet-ṭûḇî yiśbāʿû): "my people will be satisfied with my goodness" — ṭôḇ (goodness) is the same word used in v12 ("flowing toward the goodness of YHWH"). The repetition creates an inclusion framing vv10-14 as a single portrait of covenantal abundance.
15: The most cited verse of this chapter outside the New Covenant oracle itself: qôl bĕrāmâ nišmaʿ nehi bĕḵî tamrûrîm Rāḥēl mĕḇakkâ ʿal-bānêhā (qôl bĕrāmâ nišmaʿ nehi). Rāmâ was near Bethlehem in Benjamin's territory, also the site where exiles were assembled before deportation (Jer 40:1). Rachel, the mother of Benjamin and Joseph (through whom came Ephraim and Manasseh) — the matriarch of the northern tribes — weeps for children who "are no more" (ʾênennû, a permanent-state negation). Mēʾănâ lĕhinnāḥēm (mēʾănâ lĕhinnāḥēm): she refuses comfort, because comfort would require acknowledging the reality of the loss as final. Matthew 2:18 cites this text for Herod's slaughter of the innocents, reading Rachel's weeping as a typological pattern: the same grief that accompanied Israel's exile accompanies the persecution of the Messiah in his infancy.
16: Minĕʿî qôlēḵ mibbekî wĕʿênayiḵ middimʿâ (minĕʿî qôlēḵ mibbekî): YHWH's response to Rachel's weeping is a command to stop — not because the grief is unjustified, but because it is now unnecessary. Yēš śākār lĭpĕʿullātēḵ (yēš śākār lĭpĕʿullātēḵ): "there is a reward/wages for your labor" — the labor (pĕʿullâ) of bearing and raising children has not been in vain; those children will return. Wĕšāḇû mēʾereṣ ʾôyēḇ (wĕšāḇû mēʾereṣ ʾôyēḇ): the return from the land of the enemy is the reversal that transforms the weeping into hope.
17: Wĕyēš-tiqwâ lĕʾaḥărîtēḵ (wĕyēš-tiqwâ lĕʾaḥărîtēḵ): "there is hope for your future" — the term ʾaḥărît (latter end, future) in the context of hope is a key restoration term in Jeremiah (29:11: "plans for a future and a hope"). The hope is not abstract optimism but grounded in YHWH's specific promise: wĕšāḇû ḇānîm ligĕḇûlām, "your children will return to their own territory." The land promise is inseparable from the restoration hope — it is a return to the concrete inheritance.
18: The lament of Ephraim (the northern kingdom as a whole): šāmôaʿ šāmaʿtî ʾEprayim mitnôdēd (šāmôaʿ šāmaʿtî ʾEprayim mitnôdēd): the doubled infinitive absolute šāmôaʿ šāmaʿtî emphasizes YHWH's attentive hearing. Yissartanî wāʾiwwāsēr kĕʿēgel lōʾ lummad (yissartanî wāʾiwwāsēr kĕʿēgel lōʾ lummad): "You disciplined me, and I received the discipline, like an untrained calf" — the calf metaphor for an undisciplined, stubborn people (Hos 4:16: "Israel is stubborn like a stubborn heifer"); Ephraim confesses that the exile was just discipline, not capricious cruelty. hăšîḇênî wĕʾāšûḇâ (hăšîḇênî wĕʾāšûḇâ): "Restore me and I will be restored" — the classic šûḇ vocabulary, but with a theologically important structure: the repentance/return can only happen if YHWH himself initiates it. Cf. Lam 5:21 ("Return us to yourself, O LORD, that we may return"). This is the OT expression of the Augustinian/Reformed insight that even repentance is a divine gift.
19: Kî ʾaḥărê šûḇî niḥamtî (kî ʾaḥărê šûḇî niḥamtî): "After I turned away I repented/was comforted" — niḥam (Niphal of nāḥam) can mean either to repent or to be comforted; here it is genuine remorse. Wĕʾaḥărê higgāḏĕʿî sāpaḵtî ʿal-yārēḵ (wĕʾaḥărê higgāḏĕʿî sāpaḵtî ʿal-yārēḵ): "after I understood, I struck my thigh in shame" — striking the thigh is a gesture of deep grief and horror at one's own behavior (Ezek 21:12). Bōštî wĕgam niklāmtî kî nāśāʾtî ḥerpat nĕʿûrāy (bōštî wĕgam niklāmtî): the shame vocabulary is twofold — bōšet (the internal state of shame) and kĕlimmâ (public disgrace/humiliation). The exile has exposed the failure of the nation's entire early history.
20: The most tender verse in Jeremiah — YHWH's internal monologue over wayward Ephraim: hăḇēn yaqîr lî ʾEprayim (hăḇēn yaqîr lî ʾEprayim): "Is Ephraim not my dear/precious son?" — yāqîr (Piel participle of yāqar, to be precious/honored) — the question expects "yes." ʾim yeled šaʿăšûʿîm (ʾim yeled šaʿăšûʿîm): "is he not my darling child?" — šaʿăšûʿ (child of delight, darling) appears in Prov 8:30-31 for Wisdom as YHWH's delight. midday dabberî bô zākōr ʾezkĕrennû ʿôd (midday dabberî bô zākōr ʾezkĕrennû): "Even when I speak against him I still remember him" — the remembering (zākar) is covenant memory, the same zākar that YHWH exercises in Exod 2:24 of his covenant with Abraham. ʿal-kēn hāmû mēʿay lô raḥēm ʾăraḥămennû (ʿal-kēn hāmû mēʿay lô raḥēm ʾăraḥămennû): "therefore my heart is deeply moved for him; I will surely have compassion on him" — hāmâ mēʿayim is lit. "my bowels/intestines roar/churn" — the most visceral expression of emotion in Hebrew, used also in 1 Kings 3:26 for the real mother's compassion for her child. raḥēm ʾăraḥămennû: doubled infinitive absolute for absolute certainty: "I will most certainly have compassion." The root riḥam is cognate with reḥem (womb) — this is the maternal compassion YHWH uses for himself in Isa 49:15 ("Can a mother forget her nursing child... even if she should forget, I will not forget you").
21: Haṣṣiḇî lāḵ ṣiyyunîm śîmî lāḵ tamrurîm (haṣṣiḇî lāḵ ṣiyyunîm): "Set up road markers for yourself, establish guideposts" — the feminine singular imperatives address virgin Israel. Ṣiyyûn (marker, signpost) and tamrûr (bitter marker, boundary post) are both terms for landmarks along a road; in the context of exile this means remembering the way home so the return route is known. Šîtî libbēḵ lammĕsillâ (šîtî libbēḵ lammĕsillâ): "set your heart on the highway" — the mĕsillâ is the raised causeway/highway, the main road. Memory of the way home and intentional heart-orientation toward return are the twin responsibilities of the exile.
22: The most debated verse in the chapter: kî-bārāʾ YHWH ḥădāšâ bāʾāreṣ nĕqēḇâ tĕsôḇēḇ gāḇer (kî-bārāʾ YHWH ḥădāšâ bāʾāreṣ nĕqēḇâ tĕsôḇēḇ gāḇer): "For YHWH has created a new thing on earth: a female will court a man." Bārāʾ (to create) is the verb of Genesis 1:1 — a genuinely unprecedented act. The interpretive options for nĕqēḇâ tĕsôḇēḇ gāḇer include: (a) the weakened Israel (female) will now seek YHWH/Messiah (male) in a reversal of the prior rejection; (b) the newly restored nation, whose strength has been restored, will no longer need to be pursued; (c) a proverbial statement about the power-reversal of restoration. Most likely the image follows the preceding "faithless daughter" address: instead of the faithless daughter wandering (hitnôdĕdâh, v22a), she turns and pursues — the restoration reverses the direction of wandering. The context of "something unprecedented" supports a reading where YHWH's transformative act means the covenant people will be so thoroughly renewed that the typical pattern (people fleeing God) is reversed.
23: ʾOd yōmĕrû ʾet-haddāḇār hazzeh bĕʾereṣ yĕhûdâ ûḇĕʿārāyw (ʾOd yōmĕrû): "they will again say this word in the land of Judah and in its cities" — the prophetic formula of blessing will be spoken again, implying a restored land, a restored community, and restored liturgical/social speech. Yĕḇārĕkĕḵā YHWH nĕwēh-ṣedeq har haqōdeš (nĕwēh-ṣedeq har haqōdeš): "home of righteousness, O holy mountain" — the restored Jerusalem's character is defined by ṣedeq (righteousness/justice), which ch31's new covenant will produce by inward transformation. Har haqōdeš (holy mountain) is the Zion theology vocabulary of Pss 2, 48, 87.
24: Wĕyāšĕḇû ḇâ yĕhûdâ wĕḵol-ʿārāyw yaḥdāw ʾikkārîm wĕnāsĕʿû bĕʿēder (ʾikkārîm wĕnāsĕʿû bĕʿēder): farmers (ʾikkārîm) and those who move with their flocks (nāsĕʿû bĕʿēder, lit. "those who journey with the flock") — the two primary livelihoods of ancient Judah, agricultural and pastoral. Their yaḥdāw (together, united) settlement signals the end of the disruptive migrations and forced removals of war. The normalcy of daily agricultural life is itself a form of restoration.
25: Kî hirwêtî nefesh ʿăyēpâ wĕḵol-nefesh dāʾăḇâ millēʾtî (kî hirwêtî nefesh ʿăyēpâ): "For I have satisfied the weary nefesh and replenished every faint nefesh." Nefesh is the whole person — the seat of appetite, longing, vitality. ʿĀyēpâ (weary, exhausted) and dāʾăḇâ (faint, languishing) describe the depleted state of exile; hirwêtî (Hiphil of rāwâ, to drink one's fill, to be saturated) and millēʾtî (I have filled, Piel of māléʾ) are the states of complete satisfaction. This is the experiential basis of the covenant renewal — YHWH first satisfies before commanding.
26: ʿAl-zōʾt hĕqîṣōtî wāʾerʾeh ûšĕnātî ʿārĕḇâ lî (ʿAl-zōʾt hĕqîṣōtî wāʾerʾeh ûšĕnātî ʿārĕḇâ lî): "At this I awoke and looked, and my sleep had been sweet to me." A remarkably intimate aside — the prophet awakens from the vision of vv1-25 (hayyôm hazeʾ). The sweetness of the sleep suggests the vision itself was a restorative gift. This brief interruption of the oracular material frames chs 30-31 as a written vision-collection (the "Book of Consolation") deliberately set apart from the surrounding material of judgment.
27: Hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm (Hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm): the eschatological formula marking a new oracle. Wĕzāraʿtî ʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-bêt yĕhûdâ zera ʾādām wĕzera bĕhēmâ (zera ʾādām wĕzera bĕhēmâ): "I will sow the house of Israel and the house of Judah with seed of humans and seed of animals" — the fertility curse of exile (depopulation, livestock death) is reversed with an agricultural image: YHWH as farmer sowing the land with human and animal populations. Both houses together (wĕʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-bêt yĕhûdâ) — the unification of north and south continues as a theme.
28: Wĕhāyâ kaʾăšer šāqadtî ʿălêhem linĕtôš ûlintôṣ ûlĕhaʾărōš ûlĕhaʾăḇîd ûlĕhārēaʿ kēn ʾešqōd ʿălêhem liḇnôt wĕliṭṭôaʿ (kaʾăšer šāqadtî ʿălêhem linĕtôš): the šāqad wordplay reappears from 1:12 — YHWH "watches over" his word to perform it; there he was watching to destroy, now to build. The six infinitives of judgment from 1:10 (linĕtôš ûlintôṣ ûlĕhaʾărōš ûlĕhaʾăḇîd ûlĕhārēaʿ) are now answered with the two infinitives of restoration (liḇnôt wĕliṭṭôaʿ). The book's structural symmetry: what was announced in the inaugural call (1:10) is now being fulfilled in the reverse direction.
29: Bayyāmîm hāhēm lōʾ-yōmĕrû ʿôd ʾāḇôt ʾāḵĕlû ḇōser ûšinnê ḇānîm tiqhênā (ʾāḇôt ʾāḵĕlû ḇōser ûšinnê ḇānîm tiqhênā): the sour-grapes proverb also appears in Ezekiel 18:2, where it is similarly rejected. The proverb expresses the complaint of the generation experiencing exile — they are suffering for their ancestors' sins. The rejection of this proverb does not deny intergenerational consequence but announces a new principle of individual accountability in the coming age.
30: Kî ʾim-ʾîš bĕʿăwōnô yāmût (kî ʾim-ʾîš bĕʿăwōnô yāmût): "But each person will die for their own iniquity (ʿāwôn)." This is the principle of direct accountability that replaces the intergenerational blame-shifting. The phrase kol-hāʾādām hāʾōḵēl habbōser tiqhênā šinnāyw ("every person who eats sour grapes — their own teeth will be set on edge") applies the proverb to the individual: the consequence is personal to the one who commits the wrong. This prepares for the new covenant's internalized knowledge (v34) — individual relationship with YHWH means individual accountability.
32: The contrast clause: lōʾ kabbĕrît ʾăšer kāratî ʾet-ʾăḇôtām (lōʾ kabbĕrît ʾăšer kāratî): "not like the covenant I made with their fathers" — the new covenant is defined first negatively. Bĕyôm heḥĕzîqî bĕyādām lĕhôṣîʾām mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim (bĕyôm heḥĕzîqî bĕyādām): "on the day I took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt" — the intimacy of the exodus relationship, YHWH holding their hand, contrasts sharply with what follows. ʾăšer-hēmmâ hēpĕrû ʾet-bĕrîtî (ʾăšer-hēmmâ hēpĕrû): "a covenant they broke" — pārar (to break, to annul) is the technical term for covenant violation (cf. Isa 24:5; Ps 55:21). Wĕʾānōḵî bāʿaltî bām (wĕʾānōḵî bāʿaltî bām): "even though I was their husband" — baʿal as husband/master; the marriage metaphor for the Sinai covenant (cf. Jer 2:2; Hos 2:14-20). The covenant's failure is entirely Israel's — YHWH fulfilled his husband-obligations; they were the unfaithful partner.
33: The heart of the new covenant: kî zōʾt habbĕrît ʾăšer ʾeḵrōt ʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl ʾaḥărê hayyāmîm hāhēm nĕʾum-YHWH nātattî ʾet-tôrātî bĕqirbām wĕʿal-libbām ʾektĕḇennâ: the covenant is defined by two parallel verbs: nātattî (I will give/put, Qal perfect of nātan) and ʾektĕḇennâ (I will write it, Qal imperfect of kātaḇ with 3fs suffix). Nātattî ʾet-tôrātî bĕqirbām (bĕqirbām): "I will put my tôrâ within them" — qereb (inward parts, inner being) is the seat of thought and intention. Wĕʿal-libbām ʾektĕḇennâ (ʿal-libbām): "and on their heart I will write it" — the lēḇ/heart is the organ of will, understanding, and moral disposition in Hebrew anthropology. The contrast with Sinai is absolute: the stone tablets (Exod 24:12; 31:18) are replaced by the heart-tablet. The Torah is not abolished but relocated — from external code to internal disposition. Ezekiel 36:26-27 provides the mechanism: "I will give you a new heart... I will put my Spirit within you." Hebrews 8:8-12 and 10:16 cite Jer 31:33 as the definitive OT anticipation of what Christ accomplishes. Wĕhāyîtî lāhem lēʾlōhîm wĕhēmmâ yihyû-lî lĕʿām (wĕhāyîtî lāhem lēʾlōhîm): the covenant formula in its fullest, unconditional form — no "if" conditions, no conditional verb.
34: Wĕlōʾ yĕlammĕdû ʿôd ʾîš ʾet-rēʿēhû wĕʾîš ʾet-ʾāḥîw lēʾmōr dĕʿû ʾet-YHWH (lēʾmōr dĕʿû ʾet-YHWH): the mediated knowledge of YHWH that characterizes the whole prophetic office — prophets calling Israel to "know YHWH" — becomes unnecessary. Kî-ḵullām yēdĕʿû ʾôtî lĕmiqqĕṭannām wĕʿad-gĕdôlām (kî-ḵullām yēdĕʿû ʾôtî): "for they will all know me, from the least to the greatest" — kol (all) and the universal range (lĕmiqqĕṭannām wĕʿad-gĕdôlām, from the smallest to the greatest) eliminate all exceptions. The yādaʿ here is the relational, covenantal knowing of 1:5 — not merely cognitive. Kî ʾeslaḥ laʿăwōnām ûlĕḥaṭṭāʾtām lōʾ ʾezkār-ʿôd (ûlĕḥaṭṭāʾtām lōʾ ʾezkār-ʿôd): "I will forgive their iniquity and remember their sin no more" — the double negation (lōʾ... ʿôd, not... again) is absolute. The zākar (remembering) that YHWH exercises in covenant memory is here applied negatively to sin: YHWH will not bring the record of sin back into covenant relationship. Hebrews 10:17 cites this as the definitive proof that the new covenant's sacrifice (Christ's) achieved what the repeated Levitical sacrifices could not — permanent forgiveness.
35: Kōh ʾāmar YHWH nōtēn šemeš lĕʾôr yômām ḥuqqôt yārēaḥ wĕḵôḵāḇîm lĕʾôr lāylâ (nōtēn šemeš lĕʾôr yômām): the two cosmic guarantees — sun for daytime light, fixed order of moon and stars for nighttime light — are the standards by which the continuation of Israel's national existence is measured. Ḥuqqôt yārēaḥ wĕḵôḵāḇîm (the fixed orders/decrees of moon and stars) — ḥuqqôt (statutes, fixed orders) transfers the legal covenant vocabulary to the cosmic realm. Rōgaʿ hayyām wayyehĕmû gallāyw YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô (rōgaʿ hayyām wayyehĕmû gallāyw): "who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar" — the cosmic power language establishes YHWH's authority to make such a guarantee.
36: ʾIm-yāmûšû haḥuqqîm hāʾēlleh millĕpānay nĕʾum-YHWH gam-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl yišbĕtû mihyôt-gôy lĕpānay kol-hayyāmîm (ʾIm-yāmûšû haḥuqqîm hāʾēlleh millĕpānay): the conditional is deliberately impossible — yāmûšû (Qal imperfect of mûš, to depart, to be removed) applied to the cosmic fixed orders. The guarantee of Israel's continuation as a covenant people is as secure as the daily sunrise. Gam-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl yišbĕtû mihyôt-gôy lĕpānay kol-hayyāmîm: they would cease to be a nation before YHWH only if the cosmos ceased to function — an impossibility. Paul's argument in Rom 11:1-2 ("Has God rejected his people? By no means!") stands on exactly this kind of irrevocable promise.
37: Kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾim-yimmaddû šāmayim millĕmaʿlâ wĕyēḥāqĕrû môsĕdê-ʾāreṣ lĕmāṭṭâ gam-ʾănî ʾemʾas bĕḵol-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl (ʾim-yimmaddû šāmayim millĕmaʿlâ wĕyēḥāqĕrû môsĕdê-ʾāreṣ lĕmāṭṭâ): two impossibilities — the heavens above measured, the foundations of the earth below fully explored. Māʾas (to reject, despise) with bĕḵol-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl ("all the offspring of Israel") — the totality is emphatic: not partial rejection, not conditional rejection, but the total rejection YHWH says cannot happen. ʿAl kol-ʾăšer ʿāśû (ʿal kol-ʾăšer ʿāśû): "for all they have done" — even accounting for the full record of their sin, rejection is impossible. This is grace that transcends performance.
38: Hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum-YHWH wĕniḇnĕtâ hāʿîr lĕ-YHWH mimmigdal Ḥananēʾl ʿad-šaʿar happinnâ (mimmigdal Ḥananēʾl ʿad-šaʿar happinnâ): the restored city's boundaries are traced beginning at the Tower of Hananeel (NE corner of the city, Neh 3:1; 12:39; Zech 14:10) to the Corner Gate (NW, 2 Kings 14:13). The prophetic topography is precise and geographically verifiable — the promise is rooted in actual Jerusalem geography, not abstracted symbolism.
39: Wĕyāṣāʾ ʿôd qaw hamiddâ negdô ʿal-gibʿat Gārēḇ ûnāsaḇ Gōʿātâ (ʿal-gibʿat Gārēḇ ûnāsaḇ Gōʿātâ): the measuring line extends to Gareb Hill and turns toward Goah — locations not certainly identified, but probably in the west and SW of Jerusalem. The expanding perimeter suggests the restored city will be larger than its pre-exile footprint. The detailed boundary description functions as a title deed of promise, as concrete as the field purchase in ch32.
40: Wĕḵol-hāʿēmeq hapĕgārîm wĕhaddešen wĕḵol-haššĕdēmôt ʿad-naḥal qidrôn ʿad-pinnah šaʿar-hassûsîm mizrāḥâ qōdeš lĕ-YHWH (Wĕḵol-hāʿēmeq hapĕgārîm wĕhaddešen): the valley of dead bodies (pĕgārîm) and ashes (dešen) — the Hinnom Valley/Tophet, the site of child sacrifice and judgment (7:31-34; 19:6) — will be holy to YHWH. The most defiled space becomes consecrated space. The word qōdeš (holy) applied to what was the valley of slaughter is the ultimate expression of YHWH's restorative power: he does not avoid the defilement, he transforms it. Lōʾ-yinnātēš wĕlōʾ-yēhārēs ʿôd lĕʿôlām (lōʾ-yinnātēš wĕlōʾ-yēhārēs ʿôd lĕʿôlām): "it will never again be uprooted or demolished" — the six infinitives of judgment from 1:10 are permanently reversed with the two infinitives of building (v28). The city stands forever.
